ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai spicy rice noodle with chicken and vegetables. FOODON_00002187 (noodle dish) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJW (rice noodles) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for Thai spicy rice noodle with chicken and vegetables as a composite dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific Thai noodle dish. SNOMED CT coverage of regional Asian composite dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of spicy rice noodle with chicken and vegetables is approximately 350 g, typically presented as a single plate at a Thai restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Rice noodles with chicken and vegetables, stir-fried — composite estimate based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for rice noodles (cooked), chicken breast (cooked), and mixed vegetables with oil and seasoning
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Thai spicy rice noodle with chicken and vegetables as a composite dish. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data: cooked rice noodles (FDC ID 168936), cooked chicken breast without skin (FDC ID 171477), mixed stir-fried vegetables (FDC ID 169967), vegetable oil (FDC ID 172336), fish sauce (FDC ID 175148), and soy sauce (FDC ID 172238). Proportions estimated as approximately 55% rice noodles, 25% chicken, 15% mixed vegetables, 5% oil and condiments by cooked weight. Per-100g values derived from weighted average of components.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — rice noodles, boiled (GI 53)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists in the University of Sydney GI Database specifically for Thai spicy rice noodle with chicken and vegetables as a composite dish. GI of 53 is adopted from the published value for plain boiled rice noodles (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283). The addition of protein (chicken), fat (oil), and vegetables is expected to lower the effective GI of the composite dish; therefore GI 53 is a conservative upper-bound estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 56.7) / 100 ≈ 30.0, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 16.2 × 3.5 = 56.7 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced fish sauce and soy sauce, as a 350 g serving provides approximately 1470 mg sodium, representing 64–98% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 1500–2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice noodles, partially offset by protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a standard 350 g serving carries a glycemic load of approximately 30, which is in the high GL range; portion control to half a plate and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables can help moderate postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should note the high sodium content from condiments and moderate fat from cooking oil; choosing lean chicken breast, requesting less oil, and reducing soy sauce can improve the cardiovascular profile of this dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy Rice Noodle with Chicken and Vegetables is a Thai stir-fried noodle dish consisting of thin rice noodles (sen mee or rice vermicelli) cooked in a wok with sliced or diced chicken breast, mixed vegetables such as bean sprouts, cabbage, carrots, and spring onions, and seasoned with chilli, fish sauce, soy sauce, and garlic. The dish is characterised by a bold, spicy, and savoury flavour profile with a light, slightly chewy noodle texture and tender chicken pieces. It is a common menu item at Thai restaurants in Singapore, including casual dining outlets such as Sanook Kitchen. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g restaurant serving provides approximately 413 kcal, 27 g protein, 10 g fat, and 57 g carbohydrate. The dish is relatively high in sodium due to fish sauce and soy sauce, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index of rice noodles is approximately 53 (low GI), but the glycemic load per serving is high at approximately 30, requiring portion awareness for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is dairy-free and generally halal-compatible when prepared with halal-certified chicken and condiments. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to chicken and fish sauce content.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific Thai composite dish as served at Sanook Kitchen. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data using defensible proportions. GI is adopted from the closest analogous approved GI source (boiled rice noodles, University of Sydney GI Database via Atkinson et al. 2008). Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-based estimation methodology is transparent, the source data is high quality, and the proportions are consistent with standard Thai stir-fried noodle dish compositions.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component entries used for this estimation. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not consistently reported across the component entries used. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary component entries used for this estimation. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible in plant-oil-based stir-fry preparations per USDA SR Legacy data. The food_name has been normalised from the field survey menu label '714D Spicy Rice Noodle, Chicken, Vegetab' to the canonical English form 'Spicy Rice Noodle with Chicken and Vegetables'. The original menu label is preserved in aliases. Serving size of 350 g is estimated based on typical Thai restaurant noodle dish portion sizes; no exact weight was available from the field survey source. Halal status is marked true based on typical preparation at Thai restaurants in Singapore; individual restaurant certification should be verified. The food_group 'Meat and alternatives' is preserved from the input record as the dish is protein-anchored by chicken, though 'Mixed' could also be argued given the noodle base.
